question:
Let $\mathcal F$ be a finite collection of open discs in $\mathbb R^2$
whose union contains a set $E\subseteq \mathbb R^2$.  Show that there
is a pairwise disjoint subcollection $D_1,\ldots, D_n$ in $\mathcal F$
such that
\[E\subseteq \cup_{j=1}^n 3D_j.\]
Here, if $D$ is the disc of radius $r$ and center $P$, then $3D$ is the
disc of radius $3r$ and center $P$.
solution:
Define the sequence $D_i$ by the following greedy algorithm:
let $D_1$ be the disc of largest radius (breaking ties arbitrarily),
let $D_2$ be the disc of largest radius not meeting $D_1$, let
$D_3$ be the disc of largest radius not meeting $D_1$ or $D_2$,
and so on, up to some final disc $D_n$.
To see that $E \subseteq \cup_{j=1}^n 3D_j$, consider
a point in $E$; if it lies in one of the $D_i$, we are done. Otherwise,
it lies in a disc $D$ of radius $r$, which meets one of the $D_i$ having
radius $s \geq r$ (this is the only reason a disc can be skipped in
our algorithm). Thus
the centers lie at a distance $t < s+r$, and so every point at distance
less than $r$ from the center of $D$ lies at distance at most
$r + t < 3s$ from the center of the corresponding $D_i$.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $\mathcal discfamily$ be a finite collection of open discs in $\mathbb realnum^2$ whose union contains a set $targetset\subseteq \mathbb realnum^2$.  Show that there is a pairwise disjoint subcollection $firstdisc,\ldots, finaldisc$ in $\mathcal discfamily$ such that
\[targetset\subseteq \cup_{indexj=1}^{disccount} 3\iterdisc.\]
Here, if $genericdisc$ is the disc of radius $radsmall$ and center $centerpt$, then $3genericdisc$ is the disc of radius $3radsmall$ and center $centerpt$.
descriptive_long.solution:
Define the sequence $indexdisc$ by the following greedy algorithm: let $firstdisc$ be the disc of largest radius (breaking ties arbitrarily), let $seconddisc$ be the disc of largest radius not meeting $firstdisc$, let $thirddisc$ be the disc of largest radius not meeting $firstdisc$ or $seconddisc$, and so on, up to some final disc $finaldisc$.
To see that $targetset \subseteq \cup_{indexj=1}^{disccount} 3\iterdisc$, consider a point in $targetset$; if it lies in one of the $indexdisc$, we are done. Otherwise, it lies in a disc $genericdisc$ of radius $radsmall$, which meets one of the $indexdisc$ having radius $radlarge \geq radsmall$ (this is the only reason a disc can be skipped in our algorithm). Thus the centers lie at a distance $centerdist < radlarge+radsmall$, and so every point at distance less than $radsmall$ from the center of $genericdisc$ lies at distance at most $radsmall + centerdist < 3radlarge$ from the center of the corresponding $indexdisc$.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $\mathcal lanterns$ be a finite collection of open discs in $\mathbb foxgloves^2$
whose union contains a set $buttercup\subseteq \mathbb foxgloves^2$.  Show that there
is a pairwise disjoint subcollection $carnationuno,\ldots, carnationenn$ in $\mathcal lanterns$
such that
\[buttercup\subseteq \cup_{larkspur=1}^{marigolds} 3carnationjay.\]
Here, if $carnation$ is the disc of radius $glowworm$ and center $buckwheat$, then $3carnation$ is the
disc of radius $3glowworm$ and center $buckwheat$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Define the sequence $carnationith$ by the following greedy algorithm:
let $carnationuno$ be the disc of largest radius (breaking ties arbitrarily),
let $carnationdos$ be the disc of largest radius not meeting $carnationuno$, let
$carnationtres$ be the disc of largest radius not meeting $carnationuno$ or $carnationdos$,
and so on, up to some final disc $carnationenn$.
To see that $buttercup \subseteq \cup_{larkspur=1}^{marigolds} 3carnationjay$, consider
a point in $buttercup$; if it lies in one of the $carnationith$, we are done. Otherwise,
it lies in a disc $carnation$ of radius $glowworm$, which meets one of the $carnationith$ having
radius $nightfall \geq glowworm$ (this is the only reason a disc can be skipped in
our algorithm). Thus
the centers lie at a distance $silhouette < nightfall+glowworm$, and so every point at distance
less than $glowworm$ from the center of $carnation$ lies at distance at most
$glowworm + silhouette < 3nightfall$ from the center of the corresponding $carnationith$.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $\mathcal infiniteplanes$ be a finite collection of open discs in $\mathbb R^2$
whose union contains a set $wholeplane\subseteq \mathbb R^2$.  Show that there
is a pairwise disjoint subcollection $squarezoneone,\ldots, squarezonelast$ in $\mathcal infiniteplanes$
such that
\[wholeplane\subseteq \cup_{betaindx=1}^{zerocount} 3squarezoneind.\]
Here, if $squarezone$ is the disc of radius $sidelength$ and center $vertexpoint$, then $3squarezone$ is the
disc of radius $3sidelength$ and center $vertexpoint$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Define the sequence $squarezonevar$ by the following greedy algorithm:
let $squarezoneone$ be the disc of largest radius (breaking ties arbitrarily),
let $squarezonetwo$ be the disc of largest radius not meeting $squarezoneone$, let
$squarezonethree$ be the disc of largest radius not meeting $squarezoneone$ or $squarezonetwo$,
and so on, up to some final disc $squarezonelast$.
To see that $wholeplane \subseteq \cup_{betaindx=1}^{zerocount} 3squarezoneind$, consider
a point in $wholeplane$; if it lies in one of the squarezonevar, we are done. Otherwise,
it lies in a disc $squarezone$ of radius $sidelength$, which meets one of the squarezonevar having
radius $edgelength \geq sidelength$ (this is the only reason a disc can be skipped in
our algorithm). Thus
the centers lie at a distance $contactness < edgelength+sidelength$, and so every point at distance
less than $sidelength$ from the center of $squarezone$ lies at distance at most
$sidelength + contactness < 3edgelength$ from the center of the corresponding squarezonevar.

garbled_string.question:
Let $\mathcal hqmwzlcy$ be a finite collection of open discs in $\mathbb vlmxcoar^2$
whose union contains a set $yrknvduo\subseteq \mathbb vlmxcoar^2$.  Show that there
is a pairwise disjoint subcollection pjdksmza,\ldots, qagvutfj in $\mathcal hqmwzlcy$
such that
\[
yrknvduo\subseteq \cup_{pcfuvmhb=1}^{qdbrlezs} 3xydmbcen.\]
Here, if $lsyzvgta$ is the disc of radius $ofnqezsh$ and center $ermtxocn$, then $3lsyzvgta$ is the
disc of radius $3ofnqezsh$ and center $ermtxocn$.
garbled_string.solution:
Define the sequence $habqsjum$ by the following greedy algorithm:
let $pjdksmza$ be the disc of largest radius (breaking ties arbitrarily),
let $cjzarqlo$ be the disc of largest radius not meeting $pjdksmza$, let
$pkvnehts$ be the disc of largest radius not meeting $pjdksmza$ or $cjzarqlo$,
and so on, up to some final disc $qagvutfj$.
To see that $yrknvduo \subseteq \cup_{pcfuvmhb=1}^{qdbrlezs} 3xydmbcen$, consider
a point in $yrknvduo$; if it lies in one of the $habqsjum$, we are done. Otherwise,
it lies in a disc $lsyzvgta$ of radius $ofnqezsh$, which meets one of the $habqsjum$ having
radius $mdwrlaik \geq ofnqezsh$ (this is the only reason a disc can be skipped in
our algorithm). Thus
the centers lie at a distance $wrlpxtja < mdwrlaik+ofnqezsh$, and so every point at distance
less than $ofnqezsh$ from the center of $lsyzvgta$ lies at distance at most
$ofnqezsh + wrlpxtja < 3mdwrlaik$ from the center of the corresponding $habqsjum$.}

kernel_variant.question:
Let $\bigl(M^{n},g\bigr)$ be a connected, complete $n$-dimensional Riemannian manifold whose Ricci curvature satisfies  
\[
\operatorname{Ric}_{(M,g)}\;\ge\;-(n-1)\,\kappa\,g ,\qquad \kappa\ge 0 .
\tag{R}
\]

Fix any finite positive number  
\[
0<r_{0}<\infty ,
\]
and let  
\[
\mathcal F=\bigl\{\,B(x_{i},r_{i})\bigr\}_{i=1}^{N},\qquad 0<r_{i}\le r_{0},
\]
be a finite family of \emph{closed geodesic balls} whose union covers a Borel set $E\subseteq M$.

(a) (Metric Vitali-type lemma)  
Prove that there exists a pairwise disjoint subcollection  
\[
B_{1},\ldots ,B_{m}\in\mathcal F
\]
such that  
\[
E\subseteq\bigcup_{j=1}^{m}3B_{j},
\tag{A}
\]
where $3B_{j}=B\!\bigl(\operatorname{ctr}(B_{j}),3\,r(B_{j})\bigr)$.  
\emph{(The radii $3r(B_{j})\le 3r_{0}$ may exceed the injectivity radius of $M$; this causes no problem.)}

(b) (Curvature-dependent volume bound)  
Show the quantitative estimate  
\[
\operatorname{Vol}_{g}(E)
\le
C(n,\kappa,r_{0})\,
\sum_{j=1}^{m}\operatorname{Vol}_{g}(B_{j}),
\tag{B}
\]
where one can take  
\[
C(n,\kappa,r_{0})=
\sup_{0<r\le r_{0}}
\frac{V_{-\kappa}(3r)}{V_{-\kappa}(r)},\qquad
V_{-\kappa}(r)=
\begin{cases}
\omega_{n}\,r^{n}, &\kappa=0,\\[6pt]
\displaystyle
\int_{0}^{r}n\,\omega_{n-1}\bigl(\sinh(\sqrt{\kappa}\,s)\bigr)^{\,n-1}\!ds,
&\kappa>0,
\end{cases}
\]
and $\omega_{n}$ denotes the Euclidean volume of the unit $n$-ball.  
(Hint: use the Bishop-Gromov inequality and show that, when $\kappa>0$, the map
$r\longmapsto V_{-\kappa}(\lambda r)/V_{-\kappa}(r)$ is non-decreasing for every fixed $\lambda>1$.)

(c) (Near-optimality of the dilation factor)  
Prove that the multiplicative constant $3$ in {\rm(A)} cannot, in general, be replaced by any number strictly smaller than $2$: for every $\varepsilon>0$ construct a manifold $M_{\varepsilon}$ satisfying {\rm(R)} for the \emph{same} $\kappa$ together with a set $E_{\varepsilon}\subseteq M_{\varepsilon}$ and a finite family $\mathcal F_{\varepsilon}$ of geodesic balls of radii $\le r_{0}$ such that \emph{no} pairwise disjoint subcollection of $\mathcal F_{\varepsilon}$ fulfils
\[
E_{\varepsilon}\subseteq\bigcup_{j}(2-\varepsilon)B_{j}.
\tag{C}
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
\textbf{Step 0.  (Notation).}  
For a geodesic ball $B=B(p,r)$ write $\operatorname{ctr}(B)=p$ and $r(B)=r$. All distances are taken with respect to the metric $g$.

\bigskip
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Part (a) - A metric Vitali covering with factor $3$.}
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

\emph{1.  Greedy selection.}  
Re-order the balls so that $r_{1}\ge r_{2}\ge\dots\ge r_{N}$.  Build inductively a sequence $B_{1},\dots ,B_{m}$ by taking at the $k$-th step the first ball in the list that is disjoint from the previously chosen ones. When no further choice is possible the procedure stops; the selected balls are pairwise disjoint by construction.

\emph{2.  Verification of the covering property.}  
Let $y\in E$.  If $y$ already lies in $\bigcup_{j=1}^{m}B_{j}$ we are done; otherwise $y$ belongs to some ball $B=B(x,r)\in\mathcal F\setminus\{B_{j}\}$.  Since $B$ was skipped, it meets a previously selected ball, say $B_{j}=B(x_{j},R)$ with $R\ge r$.  Thus $d_{g}(x,x_{j})<r+R$.  For any $z\in B$ we have $d_{g}(x,z)\le r$, hence
\[
d_{g}(x_{j},z)
\le d_{g}(x_{j},x)+d_{g}(x,z)
<(r+R)+r\le 3R ,
\]
i.e.\ $z\in 3B_{j}$.  Taking the union over all $B\in\mathcal F$ gives (A).

\bigskip
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Part (b) - Volume estimate via Bishop-Gromov comparison.}
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

\emph{3.  Bishop-Gromov inequality.}  
Under (R) we have for every $p\in M$ and $0<r\le R$
\[
\frac{\operatorname{Vol}_{g}\!\bigl(B(p,R)\bigr)}
     {\operatorname{Vol}_{g}\!\bigl(B(p,r)\bigr)}
\le
\frac{V_{-\kappa}(R)}{V_{-\kappa}(r)} .
\tag{BG}
\]

\emph{4.  Monotonicity of model ratios.}  
Fix $\lambda>1$.  For the model space of constant curvature $-\kappa$ one checks
\[
\frac{d}{dr}\Bigl(\frac{V_{-\kappa}(\lambda r)}{V_{-\kappa}(r)}\Bigr)\;\ge\;0
\quad\text{for }r>0 .
\]
Indeed, for $\kappa=0$ the ratio equals $\lambda^{n}$.  When $\kappa>0$, write $V_{-\kappa}(r)=\omega_{n}\int_{0}^{r}\bigl(\sinh(\sqrt{\kappa}s)/\sqrt{\kappa}\bigr)^{n-1}n\,ds$ and differentiate; positivity of $\sinh$ and basic hyperbolic identities give the claim.

Consequently
\[
\frac{\operatorname{Vol}_{g}(B(p,3r_{j}))}{\operatorname{Vol}_{g}(B(p,r_{j}))}
\le
\sup_{0<r\le r_{j}}\frac{V_{-\kappa}(3r)}{V_{-\kappa}(r)}
\le
\sup_{0<r\le r_{0}}\frac{V_{-\kappa}(3r)}{V_{-\kappa}(r)} .
\tag{M}
\]

\emph{5.  Bounding $\operatorname{Vol}_{g}(E)$.}  
Using (A) and (BG) together with (M) we get
\[
\operatorname{Vol}_{g}(E)
\le
\sum_{j=1}^{m}\operatorname{Vol}_{g}(3B_{j})
\le
\Bigl(\sup_{0<r\le r_{0}}\frac{V_{-\kappa}(3r)}{V_{-\kappa}(r)}\Bigr)
\sum_{j=1}^{m}\operatorname{Vol}_{g}(B_{j}),
\]
which is precisely (B).

\bigskip
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Part (c) - Sharpness up to the constant $2$.}
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

\emph{6.  Ambient manifold.}  
Regardless of the value of $\kappa\ge 0$, the Euclidean space $\bigl(\mathbb R^{n},g_{0}\bigr)$ with the standard metric satisfies $\operatorname{Ric}_{g_{0}}=0\ge -(n-1)\kappa g_{0}$.  Hence we may set $M_{\varepsilon}:=(\mathbb R^{n},g_{0})$ for every $\varepsilon>0$.

\emph{7.  Construction of the covering family.}  
Fix $\varepsilon\in(0,1)$ and set $r:=\tfrac12\,r_{0}$.  Define three points on the first coordinate axis,
\[
p_{1}:=(-2r,0,\dots ,0),\qquad
p_{2}:=( 0 ,0,\dots ,0),\qquad
p_{3}:=( 2r,0,\dots ,0),
\]
and the corresponding closed balls
\[
B_{j}:=B_{g_{0}}\!\bigl(p_{j},r\bigr)\quad (j=1,2,3).
\]
All three balls have radius $r\le r_{0}$.  Put
\[
\mathcal F_{\varepsilon}:=\{B_{1},B_{2},B_{3}\},\qquad
E_{\varepsilon}:=B_{1}\cup B_{2}\cup B_{3}.
\]

Geometric relations:  
(i) $B_{1}\cap B_{3}=\varnothing$ because $\lvert p_{1}p_{3}\rvert=4r>2r$.  
(ii) $B_{2}$ meets each of $B_{1},B_{3}$ exactly in one boundary point since $\lvert p_{1}p_{2}\rvert=\lvert p_{2}p_{3}\rvert=2r=r+r$.

Consequently every pairwise disjoint subcollection of $\mathcal F_{\varepsilon}$ is either
\[
\{B_{1},B_{3}\},\qquad\text{or one of the singletons}\quad
\{B_{1}\},\ \{B_{2}\},\ \{B_{3}\}.
\]

\emph{8.  Failure of the $(2-\varepsilon)$-dilations.}

$\bullet$ \emph{Subcollection $\{B_{1},B_{3}\}$.}  
The centre $p_{2}$ satisfies
\[
d_{g_{0}}(p_{2},p_{1})=d_{g_{0}}(p_{2},p_{3})=2r>(2-\varepsilon)r,
\]
hence
\[
p_{2}\notin (2-\varepsilon)B_{1}\cup(2-\varepsilon)B_{3}.
\]
Therefore
\[
E_{\varepsilon}\not\subseteq(2-\varepsilon)B_{1}\cup(2-\varepsilon)B_{3}.
\]

$\bullet$ \emph{Single-ball subcollections.}  
If the chosen ball is $B_{2}$, then $p_{1}\notin(2-\varepsilon)B_{2}$ because $d_{g_{0}}(p_{1},p_{2})=2r>(2-\varepsilon)r$.  Thus $B_{1}\subseteq E_{\varepsilon}$ is not covered.  
If the chosen ball is $B_{1}$ (the case $B_{3}$ is symmetric), then again the point $p_{2}$ is uncovered, hence so is $E_{\varepsilon}$.

In all admissible pairwise disjoint subcollections the covering property (C) fails.  Hence the dilation factor $3$ in (A) cannot, in general, be lowered below $2$.

\bigskip
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Conclusion.}  
Parts (a)-(c) are proved, and the constant $3$ is therefore optimal up to the threshold value $2$.

\bigskip

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher mathematical setting â€“ From flat \(\mathbb R^{2}\) / \(\mathbb R^{4}\) we moved to an arbitrary complete Riemannian manifold with twoâ€“sided sectional-curvature bounds; geodesic rather than Euclidean balls must be handled.

2. Additional quantitative requirement â€“ Besides finding a covering we must establish the \emph{volume inequality} (2), forcing the solver to invoke the Bishopâ€“Gromov comparison theorem and to compute an explicit universal constant \(C(n,\kappa,r_{0})\).

3. Optimality component â€“ Part (c) compels the contestant to devise a counter-example proving that the enlargement factor cannot drop below \(2\), introducing a constructive extremal argument.

4. Multiple advanced techniques â€“ The full solution blends (i) greedy/Besicovitch-type selection, (ii) Riemannian triangle inequalities, (iii) curvature comparison geometry, and (iv) extremal constructions, each non-trivial on its own.

5. Increased length and depth â€“ Compared with the original disc-covering exercise, the enhanced variant demands mastery of Riemannian geometry, measure comparison theorems, and sharpness arguments, vastly expanding both the conceptual and computational workload.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let \((M^{n},g)\) be a connected, complete \(n\)-dimensional Riemannian manifold whose Ricci curvature satisfies  
\[
\operatorname{Ric}_{(M,g)}\;\ge\;-(n-1)\,\kappa\,g ,\qquad \kappa\ge 0 .
\tag{R}
\]

Choose a number  
\[
0<r_{0}<\begin{cases}
\infty , & \kappa=0,\\[4pt]
\displaystyle\frac12\operatorname{inj}(M,g), &\kappa>0,
\end{cases}
\]
and let   
\[
\mathcal F=\bigl\{\,B(x_{i},r_{i})\bigr\}_{i=1}^{N},\qquad 0<r_{i}\le r_{0},
\]
be a finite family of \textit{closed geodesic balls} whose union covers a Borel set \(E\subseteq M\).

(a)  (Metric Vitali-type lemma)  
Prove that there exists a pairwise disjoint subcollection  
\[
B_{1},\ldots ,B_{m}\in\mathcal F
\]
such that  
\[
E\subseteq\bigcup_{j=1}^{m}3B_{j},
\tag{A}
\]
where \(3B_{j}=B\!\bigl(\operatorname{ctr}(B_{j}),3\,r(B_{j})\bigr)\).

(b)  (Curvature-dependent volume bound)  
Show the quantitative estimate  
\[
\operatorname{Vol}_{g}(E)
\le
C(n,\kappa,r_{0})\,
\sum_{j=1}^{m}\operatorname{Vol}_{g}(B_{j}),
\tag{B}
\]
where one can take  
\[
C(n,\kappa,r_{0})=
\sup_{0<r\le r_{0}}
\frac{V_{-\kappa}(3r)}{V_{-\kappa}(r)},\qquad
V_{-\kappa}(r)=
\begin{cases}
\omega_{n}\,r^{n}, &\kappa=0,\\[6pt]
\displaystyle
\int_{0}^{r}\!n\omega_{n-1}\bigl(\sinh(\sqrt{\kappa}\,s)\bigr)^{n-1}\!ds,
&\kappa>0,
\end{cases}
\]
and \(\omega_{n}\) denotes the Euclidean volume of the unit \(n\)-ball.  

(c)  (Near-optimality of the dilation factor)  
Prove that the multiplicative constant \(3\) in (A) cannot, in general, be
replaced by any number strictly smaller than \(2\):  
for every \(\varepsilon>0\) construct a manifold \(M_{\varepsilon}\) satisfying (R) for the \emph{same} \(\kappa\) together with a set \(E_{\varepsilon}\subseteq M_{\varepsilon}\) and a finite family \(\mathcal F_{\varepsilon}\) of geodesic balls of radii \(\le r_{0}\) such that \emph{no} pairwise disjoint subcollection of \(\mathcal F_{\varepsilon}\) fulfils
\[
E_{\varepsilon}\subseteq\bigcup_{j}(2-\varepsilon)B_{j}.
\tag{C}
\]
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Step 0.  (Notation)  
For a geodesic ball \(B=B(p,r)\) write \(\operatorname{ctr}(B)=p\) and \(r(B)=r\).
All distances are taken with respect to \(g\).

  
Part (a) - A metric Vitali covering with factor \(3\)  
  

1.  Greedy selection.  
   Order the balls so that \(r_{1}\ge r_{2}\ge\dots\ge r_{N}\).
   Build inductively a sequence \(B_{1},\dots ,B_{m}\) by taking at the
   \(k\)-th step the first ball in the list that is disjoint from the
   previously chosen ones.  When no further choice is possible the procedure
   stops; the selected balls are pairwise disjoint by construction.

2.  Verification of the covering property.  
   Let \(y\in E\).  If \(y\in\bigcup_{j=1}^{m}B_{j}\) we are done; otherwise
   \(y\in B=B(x,r)\in\mathcal F\setminus\{B_{j}\}\).
   Since \(B\) was skipped, it meets a previously selected ball,
   say \(B_{j}=B(x_{j},R)\) with \(R\ge r\).
   Thus \(d_{g}(x,x_{j})<r+R\).
   For any \(z\in B\) we have \(d_{g}(x,z)\le r\), hence
   \[
   d_{g}(x_{j},z)
   \le d_{g}(x_{j},x)+d_{g}(x,z)
   <(r+R)+r\le 3R ,
   \]
   i.e. \(z\in 3B_{j}\).  Taking the union over all
   \(B\in\mathcal F\) gives (A).

  
Part (b) - Volume estimate via Bishop-Gromov comparison  
  

3.  Bishop-Gromov inequality.  
   Under (R) we have for every \(p\in M\) and \(0<r\le R\)  
   \[
   \frac{\operatorname{Vol}_{g}\bigl(B(p,R)\bigr)}
        {\operatorname{Vol}_{g}\bigl(B(p,r)\bigr)}
   \le
   \frac{V_{-\kappa}(R)}{V_{-\kappa}(r)} .
   \tag{BG}
   \]

4.  Bounding \(\operatorname{Vol}_{g}(E)\).  
   Using (A) and (BG) with \(R=3r_{j}\), \(r=r_{j}\) we arrive at
   \[
   \operatorname{Vol}_{g}(E)
   \le
   \sum_{j=1}^{m}\operatorname{Vol}_{g}(3B_{j})
   \le
   \Bigl(\sup_{0<r\le r_{0}}\frac{V_{-\kappa}(3r)}{V_{-\kappa}(r)}\Bigr)
   \sum_{j=1}^{m}\operatorname{Vol}_{g}(B_{j}),
   \]
   which is exactly (B).

  
Part (c) - Sharpness up to the constant \(2\)  
  

5.  Ambient manifold.  
   Regardless of the value of \(\kappa\ge 0\), the Euclidean space
   \(\mathbb R^{n}\) endowed with its standard metric \(g_{0}\) satisfies
   \(\operatorname{Ric}_{g_{0}}=0\ge -(n-1)\kappa g_{0}\).
   Hence we set \(M_{\varepsilon}:=(\mathbb R^{n},g_{0})\).

6.  Construction of the covering family.  
   Fix \(\varepsilon\in(0,1)\) and put \(r:=\tfrac12\,r_{0}\).
   Define three points on the first coordinate axis,
   \[
   p_{1}:=(-2r,0,\dots ,0),\qquad
   p_{2}:=( 0 ,0,\dots ,0),\qquad
   p_{3}:=( 2r,0,\dots ,0),
   \]
   and the corresponding closed balls
   \[
   B_{j}:=B_{g_{0}}\!(p_{j},r)\quad (j=1,2,3).
   \]
   All three balls have radius \(r\le r_{0}\).  Put
   \[
   \mathcal F_{\varepsilon}:=\{B_{1},B_{2},B_{3}\},\qquad
   E_{\varepsilon}:=B_{1}\cup B_{2}\cup B_{3}.
   \]

   Geometric relations:
   -  \(B_{1}\cap B_{3}=\varnothing\) because
      \(\lvert p_{1}p_{3}\rvert=4r>2r\).  
   -  \(B_{2}\) meets each of \(B_{1},B_{3}\) exactly in one boundary
      point since \(\lvert p_{1}p_{2}\rvert=\lvert p_{2}p_{3}\rvert=2r=r+r\).

   Consequently every pairwise disjoint subcollection of
   \(\mathcal F_{\varepsilon}\) is either
   \[
        \{B_{1},B_{3}\}
        \quad\text{or}\quad
        \{B_{1}\},\{B_{2}\},\{B_{3}\}.
   \]

7.  Failure of the \((2-\varepsilon)\)-dilations.  

   *  Subcollection \(\{B_{1},B_{3}\}\).  
      The centre \(p_{2}\) of the middle ball satisfies  
      \[
      d_{g_{0}}(p_{2},p_{1})=d_{g_{0}}(p_{2},p_{3})=2r>(2-\varepsilon)r,
      \]
      hence \(p_{2}\notin (2-\varepsilon)B_{1}\cup(2-\varepsilon)B_{3}\).
      Since \(p_{2}\) is the centre of \(B_{2}\),
      the entire ball \(B_{2}\) is missed, so (C) fails.

   *  Subcollection consisting of a single ball \(B_{j}\).  
      If \(j=2\), then  
      \(d_{g_{0}}(p_{1},p_{2})=2r>(2-\varepsilon)r\), so \(B_{1}\) is missed.  
      If \(j=1\) or \(3\), the argument above with the point \(p_{2}\)
      shows \(B_{2}\) is missed.  
      Thus (C) fails in every single-ball case as well.

   Consequently \emph{no} pairwise disjoint subcollection of
   \(\mathcal F_{\varepsilon}\) fulfils (C), establishing that the factor
   \(3\) in (A) cannot, in general, be lowered below \(2\).

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher mathematical setting â€“ From flat \(\mathbb R^{2}\) / \(\mathbb R^{4}\) we moved to an arbitrary complete Riemannian manifold with twoâ€“sided sectional-curvature bounds; geodesic rather than Euclidean balls must be handled.

2. Additional quantitative requirement â€“ Besides finding a covering we must establish the \emph{volume inequality} (2), forcing the solver to invoke the Bishopâ€“Gromov comparison theorem and to compute an explicit universal constant \(C(n,\kappa,r_{0})\).

3. Optimality component â€“ Part (c) compels the contestant to devise a counter-example proving that the enlargement factor cannot drop below \(2\), introducing a constructive extremal argument.

4. Multiple advanced techniques â€“ The full solution blends (i) greedy/Besicovitch-type selection, (ii) Riemannian triangle inequalities, (iii) curvature comparison geometry, and (iv) extremal constructions, each non-trivial on its own.

5. Increased length and depth â€“ Compared with the original disc-covering exercise, the enhanced variant demands mastery of Riemannian geometry, measure comparison theorems, and sharpness arguments, vastly expanding both the conceptual and computational workload.
